meta.title: Lab — AI experiments | Hipolit Badowski
meta.description: Progress log from a Vienna DevOps engineer using AI beyond infrastructure: RACCOON FPS (a Godot immersive sim), Blender assets over MCP, and the novel NEONFEHLER on Amazon.

lab.title: Experiments, in progress.
lab.intro: What happens when I point the same AI tooling I trust in production at things I have no business building: a cyber-noir shooter, 3D assets, and a novel. I'll post progress here as each one moves.

entries.title: Pointing AI at things that aren't infrastructure

sections.body: My day job is making infrastructure boring: predictable, reviewable, calm. Off the clock I've been doing the opposite, pointing the same AI tooling at a game engine, a 3D pipeline, and a manuscript. Three domains where I'm not an expert. The surprise was how much of the infrastructure mindset carried over, and how much of it I package back into my skill-mania repo as I go.

sections.heading: RACCOON FPS, in Godot
sections.body: RACCOON FPS is the big one: a cyber-noir immersive sim in the lineage of Deus Ex, Quake, and the early Elder Scrolls games. One raccoon, a city full of secrets, augmentations you slot in as you move. AI sits in the loop for code and iteration, but the point was never that a model writes GDScript. It's that the production habits still apply: review every change, keep a state you can roll back to, don't ship what you can't explain. A language model is a fast, confident junior. It moves quickly, and it's wrong with total conviction, so the human stays the reviewer. The two shots below sit a few weeks apart: an emoji-placeholder menu, then a 3D cyber-noir rebuild on Godot 4.7.

images.alt: Early RACCOON FPS menu with emoji level icons and a Start Game button
images.caption: RACCOON FPS · where it started

images.alt: RACCOON FPS v0.2 alpha: a 3D cyber-noir main menu with a rendered raccoon standing in a dark city
images.caption: RACCOON FPS · v0.2 alpha, Godot 4.7

sections.heading: Assets in Blender, driven over MCP
sections.body: The 3D is where AI earns its place. I drive Blender over MCP, so the model can pose, render, and export game assets while I art-direct instead of pushing every vertex. It behaves like any generated artifact: great for a first pass, still dependent on a human deciding what's actually good. Same tradeoff as generated infrastructure code, only this time the output is the raccoon.

sections.heading: NEONFEHLER, on Amazon
sections.body: The third one leaves the terminal entirely. I'm publishing a novel on Amazon: NEONFEHLER, an absurd, comic techno-fantasy. AI helped draft the book and shape its cover; it isn't the author. Same rule as the code: useful right up until you stop editing.

images.alt: Cover of NEONFEHLER by Hipolit Badowski: a neon-glitch black cat with green eyes
images.caption: NEONFEHLER · cover

sections.heading: What actually transfers
sections.body: None of this is my profession, and that's the point. What moves from infrastructure to creative work isn't a tool — it's the discipline: small steps, a human on review, a boring and reproducible process that holds whether the output is a deploy, a level, or a paragraph. What survives becomes a skill in my skill-mania repo, and I'll post progress on each of these as it moves. More soon.
